text: Emerging research indicates that the intestinal microbiota plays a critical role in modulating gut-brain communication. In this study, we investigated the specific influence of the gut microbiome on cerebral metabolism by comparing the brain metabolomic profiles of germ-free (GF) mice and Ex-GF mice (formerly germ-free mice recolonized with fecal microbiota from specific pathogen-free donors). Using capillary electrophoresis time-of-flight mass spectrometry (CE-TOFMS), we identified a total of 196 metabolites within the cerebral tissues of both groups. Quantitative analysis revealed significant differences in the concentrations of 38 metabolites between GF and Ex-GF mice. While approximately ten of these molecules have established roles in neurological function, the physiological significance of the remaining metabolites remains to be elucidated. Notably, our findings also highlight a previously unrecognized link between the intestinal microbiota and cerebral glycolytic pathways. These results demonstrate that the domestic microbiota exerts a profound influence on the cerebral metabolome via the microbiota-gut-brain axis, suggesting that microbial colonization is deeply intertwined with brain development, cognitive function, behavior, and the overall maintenance of neurological health.
